result.summary: Palantir is now the Pentagon's dominant battlefield-AI / data software vendor, spanning every service and multiple combatant commands. The flagship is the Maven Smart System (MSS), an AI command-and-control / targeting tool whose CDAO IDIQ ceiling was raised from $480M (May 2024) to ~$1.3bn through 2029 (May 2025); MSS has 20,000+ users across 35+ tools and is being designated a core/standardized DoD system. The Army is consolidating ~75 Palantir contracts into a single 10-year enterprise agreement worth up to $10bn (Aug 2025), and runs Vantage/Army Data Platform ($458M original 2019 production agreement, ~$400M follow-on Dec 2024) and the TITAN deep-sensing targeting ground station ($178M, 2024; first two units delivered Mar 2025). The Navy added a $448M ShipOS/Foundry/AIP shipbuilding-logistics deal (Dec 2025); SOCOM made Palantir lead software integrator for its Mission Command System ($111M 2021, $36.8M expansion Dec 2024); NGA awarded the $646M "Glacier Bay" contract (Sep 2025) plus a $28.3M platform-licenses deal (May 2025); Space Force took an early $10M Foundry data-backbone OTA (2021). NORTHCOM uses MSS and integrated it with Army Vantage for homeland/border-defense. Products differ: Gotham (intel/targeting), Foundry/AIP (data + LLM ops, incl. Claude integration on IL6), Maven Smart System (C2/targeting), TITAN (sensor ground station hardware+software), Apollo (deployment). KEY CONTROVERSY: Palantir's tools (and MSS specifically, which embedded Anthropic's Claude) are alleged — not court-confirmed — to enable Israeli AI targeting in Gaza and to have helped select Iranian strike targets; a UN special rapporteur found "reasonable grounds" of complicity in unlawful force. The DoD designated Anthropic a supply-chain risk in Mar 2026, forcing Claude's removal from military systems. Note: most contract values are vendor/press-reported ceilings (maximums, not obligations); NGA and CDAO figures are closer to primary procurement data. Several values (Marine Corps MSS license, exact NGA Glacier Bay scope) remain undisclosed.

deployments.customer: DoD Chief Digital and AI Office (CDAO) / multiple combatant commands
deployments.product: Maven Smart System (MSS) — AI command-and-control and targeting
deployments.what_done: AI-powered command and control for 'dynamic operations'; ingests multi-domain sensor/intel data to autonomously detect, tag and track objects/people of interest, propose targets, and accelerate the kill chain. 20,000+ active users across 35+ service/combatant-command tools. Initial five commands: CENTCOM, EUCOM, INDOPACOM, NORTHCOM/NORAD, TRANSCOM. Embedded Anthropic Claude (on Palantir AIP/AWS at IL6) for LLM tasks until DoD ordered Anthropic phased out.
deployments.value_dates: Reported: original $480M five-year IDIQ awarded May 2024 (initial $153M task order, effective June 1, 2024); ceiling raised by $795M to ~$1.3bn through 2029, announced May 21, 2025. Values reported via DefenseScoop/Breaking Defense and DoD announcements.
controversies_adjudications: MSS (with embedded Claude) reportedly helped US commanders select ~1,000 Iranian targets in the first 24 hours of strikes (Washington Post-sourced reporting) — reported, not adjudicated.

Palantir tools broadly alleged — not court-confirmed — to enable Israeli AI targeting/'kill lists' in Gaza; a UN special rapporteur found 'reasonable grounds' to believe Palantir's platform was used in unlawful use of force.
DoD designated Anthropic a supply-chain risk (Mar 4, 2026), forcing removal of Claude from military systems within six months.

solid: Maven Smart System: $480M IDIQ awarded May 2024 raised +$795M to ~$1.3bn through 2029 (announced May 2025) — confirmed via DefenseScoop and InsideDefense; ceiling-not-obligation framing is correct.

Army Enterprise Service Agreement: up to $10bn / 10 years consolidating 75 contracts (15 prime + 60 related), announced Jul 31–Aug 1 2025 — confirmed via army.mil, CNBC, Breaking Defense; correctly labeled a maximum, not an obligation.
TITAN: $178.4M Phase 3 OTA March 2024 (beat RTX/Raytheon), first two prototypes delivered March 7 2025 — confirmed via DefenseScoop, Defense News, CNBC; correctly framed as primary/reported.

NGA 'Glacier Bay' $646M firm-fixed-price awarded Sept 26 2025 AND $28.3M Platform Licenses May 5 2025 — both confirmed directly against the primary NGA Contract Announcements page; scope-undisclosed caveat is accurate.
Navy ShipOS (Foundry + AIP) up to $448M, announced Dec 9–10 2025, deployment across 2 shipbuilders / 3 shipyards / 100 suppliers — confirmed via USNI News, Breaking Defense, Business Wire.

SOCOM Mission Command System: $111M (incl. options, $52.5M on award) May 2021, plus $36.8M one-year expansion Dec 9 2024 establishing Palantir as lead software integrator — confirmed via Business Wire releases.

Army Vantage: $458M production agreement (2019) and ~$400M (actually $400.7M, $618.9M ceiling) follow-on Dec 2024, 100,000+ users — confirmed via InsideDefense, Defense News, Business Wire; 'reported' tag appropriate.
Iran-strike claim (MSS + embedded Claude reportedly helped select ~1,000 targets in first 24 hours) — confirmed as Washington Post-sourced reporting and correctly tagged 'reported, not adjudicated'.

DoD designation of Anthropic as supply-chain risk (FASCSA), forcing Claude removal — confirmed via CBS, CNBC, Mayer Brown; correctly tagged secondary/reported. Catalog's date (Mar 4 2026) and 6-month/180-day removal window match reporting.

Gaza/Israel allegations and UN special rapporteur (Albanese) 'reasonable grounds' finding — confirmed via UN report and Business & Human Rights Resource Centre; correctly tagged 'alleged / not court-confirmed', with Lavender/Gospel/Where's Daddy properly distinguished as separately-reported Israeli systems rather than Palantir products.

Product taxonomy is correctly disaggregated: Gotham (intel/targeting), Foundry/AIP (data + LLM ops), Maven Smart System (C2/targeting), TITAN (sensor ground station), Apollo (deployment) — no product conflation found.
Gaps section is honest and self-aware: it pre-flags the unverified Marine Corps MSS value, Glacier Bay scope inference, press-reported (vs primary) figures, and the un-located primary Anthropic memo.
downgrade: NGA 'Glacier Bay' product is labeled 'likely Foundry/Gotham/AIP' — correctly inferred, not stated; confidence 'medium' is appropriate and should stay medium (NGA notice does not specify scope).

Space Force entry: the initial $10M OTA (May 2021, FedScoop) is supportable, but the follow-on SSC contract modification has no pinned value/date — already flagged in gaps and rated 'medium'; keep the modification as 'reported, value/date undisclosed' rather than implying a confirmed second award.

Navy ShipOS $448M: catalog's gap note calls it 'press/business-wire reported, no primary DoD notice' — slightly understates sourcing strength (USNI News carried it), but the $448M is still an announcement/authorization figure, not a procurement-notice obligation, so the 'reported ceiling' framing is correct. No change to value needed.
Vantage follow-on labeled '~$400M' — accurate to the $400.7M obligation, but Palantir's own release headlines the $618.9M ceiling; consider noting both so the reported ceiling isn't understated.
verification.notes: Every checkable URL resolved to a real, on-topic source, and every contract value matched public reporting or primary procurement data — no fabricated URLs, invented values, or invented quotes found. The two NGA figures and the CDAO/Maven figures are correctly identified as the closest-to-primary data (NGA page is primary; verified directly). Allegations (Gaza/Lavender, Iran targeting, Anthropic complicity) are uniformly and correctly tagged 'alleged/reported, not court-confirmed.' The blocked/ended Anthropic situation is handled, though the catalog could note the additional litigation wrinkle (a federal judge initially blocked most of the FASCSA designation as punitive; the D.C. Circuit later denied Anthropic's stay so it remained in effect for covered systems) — this strengthens rather than undermines the entry. Status tags ('operational' vs 'alleged') are applied consistently and the Israel entry is correctly carved out as 'alleged' rather than 'operational.' Verdict SOLID: no fabrications, no overclaiming of reported values as primary, allegations properly hedged. The only refinements are scope/precision (Glacier Bay product inference, Space Force follow-on, Vantage ceiling), all of which the catalog already self-flags in its gaps list.

result.summary: Palantir is, by reported accounts, ICE's largest data/analytics contractor. Its footprint in US immigration enforcement spans a decade and three confirmed phases: (1) the legacy Investigative Case Management (ICM) system, built on Palantir Gotham since 2014, ICE's core case-management/investigative platform; (2) the FALCON analytic database (subject of EPIC FOIA litigation, settled 2020); and (3) the Trump-era expansion — a $30M April 2025 sole-source contract to build "ImmigrationOS" (Immigration Lifecycle Operating System), plus a reported $29.9M September 2025 maintenance task order, designed to streamline identification/apprehension of removal targets, track self-deportations in near-real-time, and manage deportation logistics. A targeting application, ELITE (Enhanced Leads Identification & Targeting for Enforcement), reported by 404 Media/Fortune in January 2026, maps deportation targets with dossiers and "address confidence scores"; it is alleged to draw on HHS/Medicaid data (the separate CMS-ICE Medicaid data-sharing agreement, July 2025, ~79M enrollees, partially blocked by a federal judge). Palantir is also reported to have built an IRS "mega-database" enabling cross-agency data linkage relevant to deportation. Litigation: EPIC v. ICE (FALCON/ICM, settled Jan 2020); Just Futures Law FOIA suit over ELITE (June 2026); EFF published a critical report (Jan 2026) which Palantir publicly rebutted. KEY DISTINCTIONS: ICM = Gotham-based case management (confirmed); ImmigrationOS = new lifecycle platform (confirmed contract, build in progress); ELITE = targeting app with alleged Medicaid-data use (reported, not officially confirmed by ICE/Palantir); the IRS data-sharing and Medicaid-data uses are the most contested/least primary-sourced elements. Reported aggregate ICE-Palantir contract value cited around $287M (2025) / $435M (2011-2026) — these totals are reported, not from a single primary notice. NOTE: I deliberately anchored load-bearing facts to ACLU, EFF, EPIC, Fortune, 404 Media, KFF, NBC, Axios, GovExec, American Immigration Council and SAM.gov rather than to unverifiable aggregator sites that appeared in results (detention-pipeline.transparencycascade.org, stateofsurveillance.org).

deployments.customer: US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) - Homeland Security Investigations
deployments.what_done: ICM is ICE's core investigative case-management platform, built on and maintained with Palantir Gotham since 2014. It links investigative records across agencies (FBI, DEA, ATF, SEVIS and others), modernizing ICE's legacy TECS system, and is used to build and manage criminal/civil detention and deportation cases.
deployments.value_dates: Original ICM award Sept 10, 2014 (reported >$41M). Current ICM contract reported ~$139.3M, awarded 2022, expiring April 2026, slated for sole-source renewal. ACLU cites ICM deal value over $145M as of 2025. Aggregate ICE-Palantir contracts reported ~$435M (2011-2026). All values reported, not from a single primary notice.
controversies_adjudications: EPIC v. ICE FOIA litigation concerning ICM/Palantir databases; ICE confirmed ICM as a sole-source Palantir system.
Sole-source justifications repeatedly assert Palantir is the 'only source' for a comparable system, drawing competition/transparency criticism.

result.summary: Palantir's identity and credibility were forged in the US intelligence community. The company was seeded in 2004 by a roughly $2M investment from In-Q-Tel, the CIA's venture arm, and from approximately 2005-2008 the CIA was its sole patron and alpha customer, co-developing what became the Gotham platform (released 2008). The best-sourced IC deployments are: CIA (Gotham, long-running, the "signature client" whose relationship soured around 2013-2015 over publicity but persisted because the software was hard to replace); FBI (Gotham, operational, though reporting describes it as a reluctant customer); and broad analyst use of Gotham across the US Intelligence Community and DoD per Palantir/Wikipedia. The NSA relationship is more nuanced than reputation suggests: Snowden-derived Intercept reporting documents Palantir tooling integrated with XKEYSCORE ("XKEYSCORE Helper") and Five Eyes use (GCHQ's "TWO FACE"), but BuzzFeed reporting shows Palantir only did pilot work for NSA that never became a full contract, with the relationship ended by about 2015. NCTC/ODNI as direct named Gotham customers are NOT cleanly sourceable as operational deployments and are dropped. DIA is currently (2026) the subject of a Palantir bid protest over the in-house MARS system rather than a deployment. The most important adjudicated event in/adjacent to this sector is Palantir's successful 2016 US Court of Federal Claims suit over the Army's DCGS-A procurement, which reshaped how the government must consider commercial off-the-shelf software. Caveat: several frequently-asserted IC customers cannot be cleanly sourced and are flagged or dropped; contract values from secondary press are marked reported.
gaps: In-Q-Tel's exact investment figure (~$2M) and 2004 date are widely reported in secondary press (BuzzFeed, Fortune, Fast Company, Wikipedia) but I could not retrieve a primary In-Q-Tel announcement or contract document confirming the precise amount/date.

No primary procurement notice (e.g., a specific contract number on USASpending or FPDS) was retrieved for the CIA or FBI Gotham work; values and existence rest on journalism and Palantir/Wikipedia statements.
NCTC and ODNI: no source found tying Palantir/Gotham to a named NCTC or ODNI deployment; commonly asserted but unsourced, so dropped.

The NSA/XKEYSCORE integration is documented via Snowden materials reported by The Intercept, but the operational scope, dates of any direct NSA contract, and whether US (vs GCHQ) analysts ran the Palantir tooling are partly ambiguous; BuzzFeed says NSA was pilot-only.
DIA MARS dispute detail (contract value, exact filing) came from search snippets; the underlying Axios scoop URL returned HTTP 403 and could not be fully verified.
Could not independently confirm whether the FBI remains a current (2026) Palantir customer; reporting indicates FBI was a top customer historically but had declined in relative ranking by FY17-FY21.

result.summary: Palantir has a deep, multi-year footprint across US federal civilian and health agencies, built primarily on its Foundry data-integration platform (with Gotham used for case/investigative analytics). The health footprint scaled massively during COVID-19: HHS Protect (hospital/COVID tracking), Tiberius (vaccine allocation), NIH's N3C COVID data enclave, and an FDA drug-review platform — later consolidated under a single $443M CDC "Common Operating Picture" contract (Dec 2022) folding in HHS Protect, Tiberius, DCIPHER and ASPR Engage. Since the March 2025 Trump executive order "Stopping Waste, Fraud, and Abuse by Eliminating Information Silos," Palantir's civilian work has become highly controversial: it is reportedly building an IRS "mega-database" (Foundry + Gotham, $130M+ since 2018) that aggregates tax, ACA, banking, crypto and FinCEN data, drawing a formal June 2025 congressional letter (Wyden/Ocasio-Cortez) alleging Privacy Act and IRC §6103/§7213A violations. Reported (NYT, May 2025) cross-agency data-linkage ambitions tie in SSA, immigration and tax records; Palantir publicly denied building a "master database," calling the NYT reporting "blatantly untrue." Newer 2025–26 civilian work includes a no-bid federal-worker attendance-tracking tool (USDA/SSA/VA), an Education Dept foreign-funding portal (as subcontractor), and a $1 CMS national provider-directory prototype. Confidence is high on the documented health contracts (procurement/SEC/agency-backed) and medium on the 2025 IRS/cross-agency "mega-database," which rests on credible but contested press reporting and a congressional letter rather than a published procurement notice naming the scope.

result.summary: Palantir's UK central-government footprint is dominated by three large, contested engagements plus several smaller ones. (1) HEALTH: the NHS Federated Data Platform (Foundry), awarded to a Palantir-led consortium in Nov 2023 - headline 'up to £330m/7 years' with a published Contract Award Notice value reported at £182.2m; operational from March 2024 but under government review with a March 2027 break clause, and the target of a Foxglove-led legal challenge over lawful basis/opt-outs (challenge ongoing, not court-confirmed as of June 2026). Its predecessor was the £1-then-£23.5m NHS COVID-19 Data Store (2020-22, ended). (2) DEFENCE: an MoD Foundry Enterprise Agreement that grew from £75.2m (Dec 2022, ended) to a £240.6m follow-on (signed Dec 2025, live from 1 April 2026, direct-awarded under a security exemption), atop a separate Sept 2025 UK-Palantir Strategic Partnership identifying up to £750m of defence opportunities over five years (with up to £1.5bn Palantir investment and London as its European defence HQ) covering AI decision-support and targeting/'kill chain' work. (3) BORDERS: a ~£27m Cabinet Office Foundry deal (Aug 2020, no tender) fusing Home Office/HMRC/Defra/DfT/DVSA/Highways/Port Health data on goods flow. Smaller/operational: the DLUHC/MHCLG Homes for Ukraine Foundry case-management system (free 6 months then ~£5.5m, ~£10m total, NAO/PAC-scrutinised) and a ~£9m Home Office/NPCC National Firearms Licensing Management System replacement (begins Sept 2026, up to 10 years). Recurring themes: direct awards without competition, a 'revolving door' of ex-MoD/Cabinet Office officials, redacted contracts, data-sovereignty and patient-data-access concerns, and rising parliamentary opposition (Early Day Motion; Hansard debates). Note: the high-profile £50m Met Police deal blocked by the Mayor (MOPAC) in May 2026 - with Palantir threatening litigation - is London policing, not central government, but is part of the same political backlash.

result.summary: Palantir's UK policing footprint as of mid-2026 spans five documented strands. (1) Metropolitan Police "Unified Data Platform" Phase 1 — an officer-profiling/anti-misconduct pilot (~£489,999, deliberately under the £500k MOPAC scrutiny threshold; ran 1 Feb–30 Apr 2026, extended) that analysed staff data (sickness, overtime, expenses, building access, complaints, device/geolocation) and reportedly led to arrests, ~98 officers assessed for IT misuse and ~500 prevention notices. (2) The separate, much larger Met "Unified Operational Analytics" (UOA) contract (up to ~£50m: ~£25.3m for 2026/27 + ~£24.8m optional extension) to automate intelligence analysis — BLOCKED by Deputy Mayor Kaya Comer-Schwartz on 20 May 2026 on procurement-rules grounds (no approved strategy, single supplier, value-for-money), with City Hall also citing Palantir's Israeli-military and US-ICE work; Palantir sent a pre-action legal letter and CEO Louis Mosley called it politicised. (3) Sadiq Khan/MOPAC then U-turned (~24-25 June 2026) granting a ~£2m, 12-month pilot extension to retain capability while a fresh open procurement runs. (4) "Project Nectar" — a Palantir Foundry-based intelligence/investigation platform piloted via Bedfordshire Police (Beds/Herts/Cambs and up to nine forces) and via Leicestershire-led EMSOU across five East Midlands forces; the EMSOU "NECTAR - EMSOU" contract was worth £818,750 (1 Oct 2024–30 Sep 2025), Home Office-funded, touching up to 80 data sources and 11 special-category data types, drawing ICO attention and MP scrutiny. (5) A national firearms/explosives/poisons licensing system — Palantir won a ~£9m (up to ~£17m incl. tax) contract via Police Digital Service/BlueLight Commercial (June 2026) to replace NFLMS across 43 forces in England & Wales, up to 10 years, beating Accenture and NEC. The Commons Science, Innovation and Technology Committee called reliance on Palantir an "unacceptable point of weakness." Product naming: Nectar/EMSOU and the operational tools are Palantir Foundry-based (Gotham not explicitly confirmed in sources); UOA is branded by Palantir as a discrete capability.

fabrication_flags: UNSOURCED VALUE — Deployment #3 (interim pilot extension) '~£2m, at least 12 months': the ~£2m figure could not be found in ANY source checked (ITV, Police Professional, Computing, Novara, The Register, Silicon UK searches). Per the rule 'reported values aren't presented as primary' and 'NEVER invent a value', this £2m must be dropped or explicitly tagged 'reported, unverified, value not disclosed by any located source' — currently it is asserted as a concrete figure.

MIS-TAGGED / DOUBLE-FRAMED EVENT — The single MOPAC-granted 12-month pilot extension is split inconsistently across Deployment #1 (which states 'MOPAC then granted a 12-month extension') and Deployment #3 (framed as a distinct 'UOA-related capability', status_tag 'operational'). Labelling #3 as UOA-related risks presenting the blocked-UOA capability as quietly revived; sources tie the extension to the existing misconduct pilot. This conflation should be corrected and the two strands reconciled.

UNVERIFIED QUOTE ATTRIBUTION — Louis Mosley 'putting politics over public safety' and Matt Cane 'outrageous and unforgivable invasion of privacy' quotes: the Register article (deployment #2 citation) does NOT contain the Mosley quote, and the Silicon UK source for the Cane quote returned 403 and could not be verified. result.summary: Palantir's documented Israel footprint centres on a strategic partnership with Israel's Ministry of Defense announced 12-13 January 2024 (board meeting held in Tel Aviv, signed amid the Gaza war), under which Palantir supplies its AI Platform (AIP) and data-analytics software. Reporting (Globes/Calcalist) frames the actual remit as logistics/manpower management and battle-management/decision-support worth "tens of millions of dollars," with Palantir's intelligence offering reportedly rejected by Unit 8200 and Shin Bet, while the MoD signed. Palantir's collaboration with Israel predates Oct 2023 (tools supplied from ~2014; Gotham reportedly used by Israeli agencies). The most serious claims — that Palantir powers the IDF targeting systems Lavender, Gospel and "Where's Daddy?" — appear in UN Special Rapporteur Francesca Albanese's report A/HRC/59/23 (released July 2025) as "reasonable grounds" findings; these are ALLEGED and NOT court-confirmed. CRITICAL DISTINCTION: Lavender, Gospel and "Where's Daddy?" are IDF-attributed systems (sourced to +972/Local Call/Guardian reporting), NOT proven Palantir products; Palantir explicitly denies involvement with Lavender and says those capabilities pre-date and are independent of its MoD partnership. CEO Alex Karp publicly said the tech kills "mostly terrorists" (April 2025). Divestment: Norway's Storebrand sold ~$24m of Palantir shares (Oct 2024); Norges Bank (the $2.3tn oil fund) backed a human-rights shareholder vote. No regulator or court has adjudicated the targeting allegations. Note: Bloomberg paywalled and the UN PDF/AFSC could not be fully fetched, so some sub-claims rest on secondary reporting and are flagged.

result.summary: Palantir's Gotham platform underpins automated police data-analysis systems in at least four German Länder, each rebranded locally: Hesse ("hessenDATA", live since 2017), North Rhine-Westphalia ("DAR", live since spring 2022), Bavaria ("VeRA", live since Dec 2024), and Baden-Württemberg (contract signed March 2025, ~€25m, expected live ~2026). Bavaria's €25m framework agreement (2022) was structured so other federal/state forces could buy in without separate procurement. The decisive legal event is the Federal Constitutional Court (BVerfG) judgment of 16 Feb 2023 (1 BvR 1547/19 Hesse; 1 BvR 2634/20 Hamburg), which struck down the "automated data analysis" provisions (§25a HSOG Hesse; §49 HmbPolDVG Hamburg) as violating the right to informational self-determination. Hamburg's provision (which had never been used to deploy Palantir) was declared void; Hesse's was declared incompatible but allowed to continue until a corrected law by 30 Sep 2023. The ruling explicitly discusses Palantir's Gotham/hessenDATA. NGOs (GFF / Gesellschaft für Freiheitsrechte, with Chaos Computer Club) brought the original complaints and filed a fresh constitutional complaint against Bavaria's VeRA (Art. 61a BayPAG) on 23 July 2025. Separately, the federal domestic intelligence agency BfV chose French ChapsVision (ArgonOS) over Palantir, and the BKA federal procurement has been slowed — i.e. Palantir has NOT won the German federal level. Reported usage shows mission creep: systems pitched for terrorism/organised crime are used for property/minor crimes (e.g. bicycle theft, ATM burglaries).

result.summary: Palantir has become the dominant Western software vendor inside Ukraine's war effort since CEO Alex Karp visited Kyiv in June 2022 and offered to deploy the company's software, opening an office in the capital. Documented deployments cluster in four areas: (1) battlefield targeting -- Karp has publicly claimed Palantir software is "responsible for most of the targeting in Ukraine," with Gotham fusing drone/satellite/intercept/civilian-app data and MetaConstellation tasking commercial-satellite swarms; the newer PRISMA platform is reported (CNN, May 2026) to support military intelligence (HUR/GUR) deep-strike drone operations into Russia. (2) War-crimes evidence -- a 2023 agreement (Palantir Technologies UK Ltd) with the Prosecutor General's Office to structure evidence for 78,000+ registered war crimes. (3) Demining -- a March 2024 partnership with the Ministry of Economy to prioritise mine clearance, coordinating with humanitarian operators including the HALO Trust. (4) Reconstruction/economic and education data -- a May 2023 MoU with the Ministry of Digital Transformation using Foundry to catalogue war damage and optimise reconstruction; the Ministry of Education uses Foundry for school-safety data. Ukraine signed memoranda with the Defence, Digital Transformation, Economy and Education ministries; in May 2026 Zelenskyy/Defence Minister Fedorov and Karp agreed to expand cooperation around the Brave1 cluster. Caveats: much early support was reportedly free of charge; contract values are largely undisclosed; the PRISMA deep-strike role is press-reported (CNN) and not confirmed by a Palantir release; and security analysts (citing a Swiss 2024 audit) have flagged risks of US-government/intelligence access to Ukrainian data, heightened under the Trump administration. No court or regulator has adjudicated these Ukraine-specific deployments.
gaps: Contract values for all Ukraine ministry deals are undisclosed; reporting (TIME) says early support was provided free of charge, but no figure or duration is documented.

The exact Palantir product behind battlefield 'targeting' is not always specified in primary sources; Gotham and MetaConstellation are named in reporting but Palantir press releases on targeting are sparse for OPSEC reasons.
PRISMA's role in HUR/GUR deep-strike operations rests on CNN's May 2026 reporting with unit access; no Palantir press release or procurement notice confirms it.
The May 2026 Zelenskyy-Fedorov-Karp 'data centre'/Brave1 agreement is reported via Ukrainian/Russian-mirror press; scope, value and signed-contract status are unclear.
Whether HALO Trust has a direct contractual relationship with Palantir (vs. Palantir's demining platform merely coordinating HALO teams) is not clearly documented in primary sources.
No independent confirmation of which ministries pay vs. receive software gratis; the four-ministry MoU framing comes from secondary reporting (UNITED24).

result.summary: Palantir's documented Asia-Pacific footprint is concentrated in three countries: Japan, Australia, and South Korea, with smaller/older traces in New Zealand. The flagship commercial deployment is SOMPO Holdings in Japan (Foundry/AIP across insurance and senior-care, ~8,000 users, multi-year expansions since 2020). In South Korea, HD Hyundai (Hyundai Heavy Industries) runs Foundry/AIP across shipbuilding and heavy industry, expanded in Jan 2026 to a reported "hundreds of millions" multi-year deal. In Australia, Palantir is embedded across the federal national-security ecosystem: Department of Defence (Foundry for supply-chain/IIC, and a 2026 ICT platform for the Cyber and Electronic Warfare Division), AUSTRAC (transaction-reporting/AML platform, largest tech supplier with >A$28M), and the ACIC (Gotham, intelligence linkage of ~42M data points) — with significant reporting on non-competitive/limited tenders and the federal government's equity stake in Palantir. New Zealand had an NZDF Gotham relationship (2012-2018+) and a short NZ Police social-media analysis trial after the 2019 Christchurch attack that ended (price); the current NZ govt says it has no plans to expand Palantir in the defence-tech space. Singapore: NO documented Palantir government deployment was found in public sources — only Palantir's general government web offerings and unrelated MINDEF references; treat Singapore as undocumented. The Japan "Centene" lead refers to a US healthcare entity and is out of APAC scope. Most contract values for Korea/Japan are company-reported (press releases), not primary procurement notices; Australian figures derive from AusTender/news reporting and NZ figures from OIA/Ombudsman disclosures.

result.summary: Palantir's documented Middle East/Gulf footprint is concentrated in the UAE (commercial/civil) and Israel (defense). The flagship Gulf item is Aither, Palantir's first UAE joint venture, launched with state-owned Dubai Holding (signing 31 Oct 2025, announced 4 Nov 2025), formalizing ~18 months of prior Foundry/AIP deployment across Dubai Holding's real estate, hospitality, finance and infrastructure operations (brands Nakheel, Meraas, Jumeirah), with stated intent to expand to UAE commercial and civil-government clients. UAE-headquartered AppliedAI (founded by Arya Bolurfrushan) counts Palantir, G42 and Mubadala among its investors/backers, but this is an equity stake, not a documented Palantir product deployment. In Israel (Middle East), Palantir signed a strategic partnership with the Israeli Ministry of Defence in January 2024 to support the war effort; press reporting (denied by Palantir) alleges its infrastructure underpins AI targeting in Gaza — these allegations are NOT court- or regulator-confirmed and Palantir explicitly denies involvement with Lavender/Gospel. Palantir was also publicly acknowledged by Qatar's National Cyber Security Agency among 2022 World Cup partners, though the specific role is undisclosed. Evidence for Saudi Arabia (beyond Palantir products being available on Oracle Cloud sovereign regions), Bahrain, Kuwait and Oman government deployments is thin to nonexistent in public sources and was dropped. Note: ownership split and contract values for Aither were NOT disclosed; treat all monetary figures as reported, not from primary procurement notices.

result.summary: This is the running tally of formal challenges, regulator actions, court rulings, and campaigns touching Palantir deployments worldwide. It is meta to the other sectors: each item points back at a deployment catalogued elsewhere (German state police / Gotham-VeRA, Europol / Gotham, NHS England / Foundry FDP, ICE / ICM-FALCON, IDF / AIP, Canadian federal COVID work). The strongest, court-confirmed item is the German Federal Constitutional Court's 16 Feb 2023 ruling striking down the automated-data-analysis powers underlying Hesse's and Hamburg's Palantir use; a follow-on wave of constitutional complaints (GFF/CCC) against Bavaria (VeRA) and North Rhine-Westphalia (DAR) is pending at the same court. Regulator action: the EDPS ordered Europol (a long-time Gotham user) to delete unlinked personal data (Jan 2022). In Canada, the federal Ethics Commissioner and Lobbying Commissioner separately found Palantir Canada president David MacNaughton breached conflict-of-interest and lobbying rules over pro-bono COVID offers. In the UK, London Mayor Sadiq Khan via MOPAC blocked the Met's ~£50m Palantir deal (2026) citing a procurement breach, prompting a Palantir pre-action legal threat; and the Good Law Project / Foxglove pursued legal action over the redacted £330m NHS Federated Data Platform contract, winning partial de-redaction. Campaign/divestment pressure: Norway's NBIM (GPFG), backed by a coalition of ~$336bn AUM, voted FOR a human-rights impact assessment at Palantir's 3 June 2025 AGM, but the dual-class structure (Thiel/Karp/Cohen up to ~49.999% voting) defeated the proposal; ABP (Netherlands) and Storebrand (Norway) divested; NY State Comptroller DiNapoli refused to divest. Allegation layer (NOT court-confirmed): UN Special Rapporteur Francesca Albanese's 2025 'economy of genocide' report named Palantir, alleging 'reasonable grounds' it supplied AIP/predictive-policing/defence infrastructure linked to IDF Lavender/Gospel/Where's Daddy targeting; Palantir denies involvement in Lavender/Gospel. EPIC's earlier FOIA litigation (EPIC v. ICE) exposed Palantir's ICM/FALCON capabilities; American Oversight sued for records on 2025 Trump-admin Palantir use. All contract values are reported unless tied to a primary procurement notice.

deployments.customer: Land Hesse police & Land Hamburg police (challenged before Federal Constitutional Court / BVerfG)
deployments.product: Gotham (deployed in Hesse as 'hessenDATA' since 2017; Hamburg analogue)
deployments.what_done: Automated cross-database analysis of stored police personal data to predict/prevent crime. BVerfG First Senate, 16 Feb 2023 (1 BvR 1547/19, 1 BvR 2634/20), held the enabling powers (HSOG §25a(1) 1st alt.; HmbPolDVG §49(1) 1st alt.) unconstitutional for violating informational self-determination by lacking sufficient interference thresholds. Hamburg provision declared void immediately; Hesse provision allowed to continue under restrictions only until 30 Sep 2023 pending new legislation.

fabrication_flags: CANADA / Lobbying Commissioner finding is MISSTATED (factual fabrication of the outcome). The catalog says the Commissioner of Lobbying 'found 31 undisclosed/registrable communications, breaching lobbying rules.' The actual report (https://lobbycanada.gc.ca/media/ue0cr5g5/investigation-report-david-macnaughton-2021-03-22-en.pdf, dated 2021-03-22) CONCLUDES THE OPPOSITE: of 49 communications, 31 were pro-bono offers that 'do not relate to any subject matter under the Lobbying Act' and were therefore NOT subject to the restriction; the Commissioner explicitly determined MacNaughton 'did not contravene the five-year restriction on lobbying.' The Logic's own headline: 'investigation finds Palantir's MacNaughton didn't break rules.' Only the separate Ethics Commissioner (Section 41 order, 16 Sep 2020) made an adverse finding. The 'two separate federal regulator findings against' / 'breaching lobbying rules' framing is unsupported and must be corrected: the Lobbying finding was a NON-breach.

AGM DATE WRONG BY ONE YEAR (fabricated/incorrect date presented as fact). The NBIM-led human-rights-audit item, the summary, and value_dates all state the AGM was '3 June 2025.' Primary/secondary sources place it on 3 June 2026: Quartr 'Palantir AGM 2026', Bloomberg (2026-05-29 'Norway Wealth Fund Backs Human Rights Review at Palantir'), TNW, IASJ ('Palantir Non-Insider Vote, a Resounding Victory' — 56% of non-insiders voted for Proposal 5), and SEC 8-K pltr-2025[date]. Also the summary's 'NY State Comptroller DiNapoli refused to divest' and the divestment timeline are internally consistent only if 2026 is used. Correct 2025 -> 2026 everywhere this AGM is referenced.

verification.notes: Overall the catalog is sourced with real, checkable URLs (BVerfG, EDPS, lobbycanada.gc.ca, foxglove, UN unispal, heise, BHRRC, NL Times, City AM all verified live) and is unusually disciplined about tagging alleged-vs-confirmed and reported-vs-primary; the gaps section is honest. title: Rest of Europe & EU institutions (excluding Germany, covered separately)

result.sector: Rest of Europe & EU institutions (excluding Germany)
result.summary: Palantir's footprint across Europe-ex-Germany and EU bodies spans intelligence, policing, defence, and pandemic response, with a marked recent reversal driven by sovereignty and human-rights concerns. EU-institution use centred on Europol, which ran Palantir Gotham (via a 2012 Capgemini-awarded "Europol Analysis System" contract) for operational/counter-terror analysis until Europol says it terminated the licence in 2021; the EDPS's January 2022 erasure order against Europol (datasets lacking Data Subject Categorisation) is the major adjudication, though it does not name Palantir. National policing: Denmark's POL-INTEL (Gotham, won via 2016 tender, operational) is the clearest live deployment; Norway's Project Omnia (Gotham, bought 2016) FAILED and was cancelled in 2020. Intelligence: France's DGSI ran Palantir since 2016 (renewed 2022 and Dec 2025) but in June 2026 announced it will replace Palantir with French ChapsVision (ArgonOS) on sovereignty grounds, triggered by a US restriction on foreign access to Anthropic's "Fable" model — Palantir tools stay live during a 1-3 year migration. Defence: Poland signed a letter of intent (Oct 2025) for data/AI/cybersecurity (value undisclosed; early-stage). Pandemic-era Foundry deployments in the Netherlands (safety regions COP) and Greece (PM dashboard, controversial, no procurement registration/DPIA) were operational in 2020-21. Ukraine's Prosecutor-General's Office signed with Palantir UK (2023) for war-crimes evidence processing (pro bono). Investor backlash is a distinct strand: Dutch fund ABP divested its ~€825m stake (Q4 2025); Norway's Storebrand divested ~$24m (2024); Norway's $2.3tn sovereign wealth fund (NBIM) voted FOR a human-rights review at the June 2026 AGM. No sourced confirmation of a direct Frontex-Palantir contract was found (Frontex is reported to use Microsoft BI; Palantir-Frontex remains unverified).

deployments.customer: Europol (European Union Agency for Law Enforcement Cooperation)
deployments.what_done: Operational/counter-terrorism analysis: data fusion and network analysis across phone records, social media, images and metadata. Gotham entered Europol's core 'Europol Analysis System' via a 2012 contract awarded to Capgemini; Europol used Gotham for 'operational analysis' alongside an iBase repository. Europol states it terminated the Palantir/Gotham licence in 2021.
deployments.value_dates: Software embedded via 2012 Capgemini-awarded contract; in use through ~2016-2021; Europol says licence terminated 2021. No public contract value for the Palantir component.
controversies_adjudications: EDPS inquiry (opened 2019) led to a 3 Jan 2022 order requiring Europol to erase datasets lacking Data Subject Categorisation (6-month categorise-or-erase for new data; 12 months for legacy data) with quarterly reporting; the order does not name Palantir.
A later Europol Regulation amendment retroactively legalised the data, prompting EDPS to take legal action (the action was later ruled inadmissible by the General Court).
Transparency: journalists seeking documents on Europol-Palantir received only 2 of 69; disclosures showed Europol had considered legal action against Palantir.

result.summary: Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ: PLTR) is a US data-analytics/software company founded May 2003 by Peter Thiel, Alex Karp, Stephen Cohen, Joe Lonsdale and Nathan Gettings, with early-stage backing (~$2M) from In-Q-Tel, the CIA's venture arm. Per its FY2024 10-K (filed Feb 2025), total revenue was ~$2.866B, up 29% YoY: government ~55% (~$1.57B, +28%) and commercial ~45% (~$1.30B, +29%); US ~66% of revenue vs ~34% international. Customer count reached 711 at year-end 2024, up from 497 in 2023. Founders retain ~49.99% voting control via Class F super-voting stock held in the Thiel/Karp/Cohen Founder Voting Trust (trustee Wilmington Trust). Products: Gotham (government/intelligence), Foundry (commercial), Apollo (deployment layer), AIP (AI layer). Federal lobbying rose from ~$2.4M (2020) to ~$5.88M (2024) and ~$6.08M (2025) per OpenSecrets. Market cap fluctuated dramatically, reportedly reaching ~$430B in 2025 (after S&P 500 inclusion Sept 2024) before a 2026 drawdown. Market sentiment/context: Michael Burry's Scion Asset Management disclosed Q3 2025 put options on PLTR (headline ~$912M notional, but Burry clarified actual premium ~$9M); Scion deregistered/liquidated late 2025. Significant controversies: ICE ImmigrationOS, NHS Federated Data Platform, blocked Met Police deal, and a UK "revolving-door" investigation (30+ ex-officials hired). NOTE: sourced market context, NOT investment advice. CONFLICT: Wikipedia rendered FY2024 revenue as $2.23B; the SEC 10-K-sourced figure (~$2.866B) is authoritative and used here.

result.summary: Palantir's documented footprint in the humanitarian/international-organisation space rests on one confirmed, named institutional customer: the UN World Food Programme (WFP), which in February 2019 entered a five-year partnership using Palantir Foundry for supply-chain optimisation, cash-based transfers and operational data integration. WFP's own press release and CIO statement confirm the product (Foundry), the prior pilot tool (Optimus), and savings figures (>US$30m realised, up to US$100m potential). The widely cited "US$45 million / five-year" contract VALUE is reported (Privacy International, The New Humanitarian, Geneva Solutions), NOT stated in WFP/Palantir primary materials. The deal triggered a 65-signatory open letter (Responsible Data / Privacy International) over risks to ~90 million aid recipients; WFP responded that Palantir would not access beneficiary-identifiable data or conduct data mining. Advocacy sources (Privacy International) later characterised Palantir as "the cornerstone of the WFP data ecosystem" and report the partnership was extended beyond its original term — these are advocacy/reported characterisations, not procurement-confirmed. Separately, a single-outlet 2026 investigation (Drop Site News) reports Palantir holds a permanent desk at the US-led Civil-Military Coordination Center (CMCC) in Israel tracking Gaza aid convoys via Foundry, with alleged Foundry-Gotham interoperability — this is reported/alleged. Leads on WHO, UNHCR, IOM and a purported MSF/Doctors Without Borders refusal of Palantir did NOT surface checkable Palantir contracts/facts and are dropped or flagged as gaps. (Note: Palantir's COVID work with US NIH/CDC/HHS and the UK NHS is government-health, not humanitarian/IO, so excluded from this sector.)
